annotations.text: The information we have about the patient's symptoms suggests that the bleeding originated in the bladder.
She had some dysuria, which hints at a source in the lower urinary tract.

Bleeding from the upper tract, in contrast, might have been associated with flank pain due to ureteral colic or with the passing of vermiform clots (squeezed into that shape during passage down the ureter).
In addition, the dysuria and the presence of white cells in the urine hint at an irritative process in the bladder.
If the cause was bacterial, her symptoms might have been expected to improve after she had taken trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole for a few days.

A good bimanual pelvic examination in a woman of normal body habitus whose bladder is empty can go a long way toward ruling out an infiltrating bladder cancer, which might be manifested as a mobile or fixed mass or as an area of induration.

On cystoscopic examination, the finding of clear urine coming from both ureteral orifices would help to confirm that the bleeding was in fact coming from the lower urinary tract rather than the upper tract.
The CT scan in the current case does not suggest a lesion in the usual site of a urachal adenocarcinoma.
The differential diagnosis of a bleeding mass in the bladder in a 33-year-old woman includes both malignant tumors and nonmalignant tumors.
Because of its frequency, transitional-cell carcinoma has to be placed high on the list of probable diagnoses, even in a 33-year-old patient.
By a process of elimination, my diagnosis in this patient would be either transitional-cell carcinoma of the bladder or inflammatory pseudotumor of the bladder.

On the basis of probability, transitional-cell carcinoma would be the more likely diagnosis, but the young age of the patient, the absence of any conditions that might predispose her to transitional-cell carcinoma, and her sex, as well as the fact that this case was considered interesting enough to be selected for presentation at a clinicopathological conference, lead me to a diagnosis of inflammatory pseudotumor of the bladder.
The lesion most closely related to the inflammatory pseudotumor is the postoperative spindle-cell nodule.
It has been demonstrated that positive staining for anaplastic lymphoma kinase is typically seen in cases of inflammatory pseudotumor.

Microscopically, this lesion usually has an edematous, polypoid appearance that is quite different from that of a papillary carcinoma; however, when there are more delicate papillary structures, the differential diagnosis with a papillary carcinoma may not be straightforward on pathological examination.
Patients with tuberculosis may present with gross hematuria, but it would be unusual for hematuria to be the very first symptom of tuberculosis.
In a patient with hematuria, persistent sterile pyuria, and an acidic urinary pH, tuberculous cystitis should be considered.
